name: philosophy_of_mathematics
assumptions: Mathematical objects (numbers, sets, structures) have a mode of existence that demands philosophical explanation
Mathematical truth differs in kind from empirical truth; its epistemological basis is distinct
Mathematical practice includes proof, definition, axiomatisation, and conceptual innovation—not just formal derivation
Different branches of mathematics raise different philosophical problems (set theory, category theory, geometry, computation)

norms.message: Should evaluate the type and standards of mathematical proof
Should address formal consistency and relative consistency proofs
Should consider completeness and incompleteness results
Should engage with foundational frameworks (ZFC, NF, HoTT, etc.)

bridge_warnings.explanation: Mathematical abstraction removes irrelevant properties to expose formal structure; software abstraction hides implementation detail behind interfaces. The former is discovery-oriented; the latter is design-oriented. Direct conflation risks category error.

Mathematical proof is a social-epistemic practice involving understanding and explanation; formal verification is a mechanical procedure. The latter can guarantee correctness but does not constitute mathematical understanding.
